coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive fluorescence immunosensor for SCCA detection based on Ag NPs@N-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zhirou
Wu, Zhengtong
Sun, Xuyang
coredata.dc:description: An ultrasensitive fluorescence immunosensor based on silver nanoparticles@ nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (Ag NPs@N/CQDs) and exploiting the quenching effect of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) was fabricated for squamous cell carcinoma antigen (SCCA) detection. Ag NPs@N/CQDs were immobilized on the surface of an indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode and then coupled with the primary antibody of SCCA. The primary antibody captured SCCA on the ITO electrode, forming an immune complex with the secondary antibody labeled with gold nanoparticles, thereby constructing a sandwich-structured fluorescent sensor. Using the prepared sensor, a new method for detecting trace amounts of SCCA in human serum was established. Owing to the effective quenching of fluorescence intensity of Ag NPs@N/CQDs by Au NPs, combined with the presence of a sandwich immune structure, the sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity. The method demonstrates a linear range of 2 − 4000 fg mL−1 and a detection limit of 0.87 fg mL−1. The the recovery rate for real serum samples was between 94.45 % to 103.43 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Au NPs
Immunosensor
SCCA
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of Ag NPs@N/CQDs and Au NPs
Fluorescent response of the immunosensor to SCCA
Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the immunosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the First Clinical Medical School, Hainan Medical University, China, and Department of Ophthalmology, Hainan Provincial Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the construction of the fluorescence immunosensor.

(A) TEM image of Ag NPs@N/CQDs; (B) Particle size distribution of Ag NPs@N/CQDs; (C) XRD pattern of Ag NPs@N/CQDs; (D) XPS spectrum of Ag NPs@N/CQDs; (E) SEM image of Au NPs; (B) Particle size distribution of Au NPs; (C) XRD pattern of Au NPs.

CV (A) and EIS (B) of the immunosensor preparation process: a. ITO electrode; b. Ag NPs@N/CQDs modified ITO electrode; c. Ag NPs@N/CQDs/primary antibody modified ITO electrode; d. Ag NPs@N/CQDs/primary antibody/SCCA modified ITO electrode; e. Ag NPs@N/CQDs/primary antibody/SCCA/secondary antibody modified ITO electrode.

(A) Fluorescence quenching of the immunosensor upon adsorption of different concentrations of SCCA. The SCCA concentrations from a to d are: 0, 2, 200, and 2000f g L−1, respectively. (B) Optical spectrum of nanophase materials: a. emission spectrum of Ag NPs@N/CQDs; b. absorption spectrum of Au NPs.

(A) Fluorescent response of the immunosensor to different concentrations of SCCA. a-l: 0, 2, 5, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 3000, and 4000 fg L−1. (B) Calibration curve for the immunosensor detecting SCCA.
Sample test result and recovery experiments with the calibration method.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive fluorescence immunosensor for SCCA detection based on Ag NPs@N-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The First Clinical Medical School, Hainan Medical University, Haikou 570102, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The First Clinical Medical School, Hainan Medical University, Haikou 570102, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Ophthalmology, Hainan Provincial Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Haikou 570203, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hainan Provincial Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Ophthalmology, Hainan Provincial Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Haikou 570203, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the construction of the fluorescence immunosensor.

An ultrasensitive fluorescence immunosensor based on silver nanoparticles@ nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (Ag NPs@N/CQDs) and exploiting the quenching effect of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) was fabricated for squamous cell carcinoma antigen (SCCA) detection. Ag NPs@N/CQDs were immobilized on the surface of an indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode and then coupled with the primary antibody of SCCA. The primary antibody captured SCCA on the ITO electrode, forming an immune complex with the secondary antibody labeled with gold nanoparticles, thereby constructing a sandwich-structured fluorescent sensor. Using the prepared sensor, a new method for detecting trace amounts of SCCA in human serum was established. Owing to the effective quenching of fluorescence intensity of Ag NPs@N/CQDs by Au NPs, combined with the presence of a sandwich immune structure, the sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity. The method demonstrates a linear range of 2 − 4000 fg mL−1 and a detection limit of 0.87 fg mL−1. The the recovery rate for real serum samples was between 94.45 % to 103.43 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Squamous cell carcinoma antigen (SCCA) [1], a serological marker for advanced squamous cell tumours, exhibits elevated levels in human serum that are closely associated with malignancies such as cervical cancer, squamous cell lung cancer, and oesophageal cancer. Therefore, the detection of SCCA in human serum is critical for the early screening of these cancers. Current SCCA detection methods include enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [2], colorimetric assays [3], and electrochemical sensing [4]. However, existing methods are limited in their ability to detect trace amounts of SCCA in human serum. Specifically, ELISA and colorimetric assays lack the sensitivity required for trace-level detection, while electrochemical sensors require further improvements in stability and reproducibility. Therefore, the development of novel methodscapable of achieving highly sensitive and stable detection of trace SCCA in human serum holds significant clinical importance. Fig. 1 .

Fluorescence analysis [5,6] has emerged as a promising tool in immunoassays due to its high sensitivity, low cost, rapid response and operational simplicity. Fluorescent reagents, as the core components of fluorescence-based detection platforms (e.g. fluorescent sensors), primarily include traditional organic dyes [7], and metal-based quantum dots (QDs) [8]. However, the practical applications of conventional fluorescent reagents are limited by their poor biocompatibility, low quantum yield, and inherent toxicity. Furthermore, traditional fluorescent sensors exhibit insufficient specificity for target molecules and require enhanced anti-interference capabilities. To address these challenges, fluorescent sensors with improved biocompatibility, high quantum yield, low toxicity, and selective target molecule recognition must be developed.

Recently, novel fluorescent nanomaterials, such as carbon QDs (CQDs) [9,10], have attracted considerable attention. CQDs, zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with exceptional fluorescent properties, are characterized by their small molecular weight, nanoscale dimensions, photostability, broad excitation spectra, tuneable emission wavelengths, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity [11]. These attributes enable their application in the detection of cancer biomarkers [12,13], ions, [14,15] and hazardous substances in food [16,17]. Despite their unique optical properties, the practical utility of CQDs is hindered by their relatively low fluorescence efficiency. Nitrogen doping is an effective strategy to enhance the fluorescence efficiency of CQDs [18]. In addition, metal or metal oxide nanomaterials and other non-polar nano materials exhibit advantages such as good biocompatibility, high specific surface area, and surface functionalization [19–21]. Integrating CQDs with inorganic nanomaterials not only improves their stability in aqueous solutions but also substantially augments their luminescent performance [22].

To enhance the selectivity of fluorescence sensors, incorporating molecular recognition elements is essential. Commonly used elements include antibodies [23], DNA aptamers [24,25], and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) [26]. DNA aptamers provide limited chemical binding forces compared with antibodies (composed of 20 amino acids), making them unsuitable for targets with low binding affinity. MIPs are constrained by their low imprinting capacity, poor stability, and inconsistent reproducibility. Meanwhile, antibodies exhibit superior specificity, stability, and reliability due to their inherent ability to target immunogenic substances, ease of functionalisation, and compatibility with signal amplification strategies [27]. Thus, immunosensors that leverage antibody-based recognition hold immense potential for clinical diagnostics, particularly in cancer biomarker detection.

Herein, we synthesized a silver nanoparticles@nitrogen-doped CQDs (Ag NPs@N/CQDs) core–shell fluorescent nanomaterial using pineapple leaves as a carbon source via pyrolysis. Ag NPs@N/CQDs were immobilized onto an indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode using a crosslinking agent, followed by conjugation of the SCCA primary antibody via glutaraldehyde. Upon capturing SCCA on the ITO surface, a sandwich immunocomplex was formed by the binding of the antigen to Au NP–labelled secondary antibody. The strong fluorescence emission of Ag NPs@N/CQDs is effectively quenched by Au NPs via electron-transfer mechanisms; specifically, Au NPs transfer electrons to excited-state N/CQDs, thereby suppressing radiative recombination. This quenching effect correlates with the SCCA concentration, enabling quantitative detection. The prepared fluorescence sensor demonstrates high sensitivity and specificity, attributed to the high quantum yield of Ag NPs@N/CQDs and selective recognition provided by the sandwich immunoassay architecture. In this work, we utilized inexpensive pineapple leaves as raw materials to prepare N/CQDs fluorescent probes with high luminescence performance, effectively reducing costs. The introduction of Ag NPs improved the water solubility and stability of N/CQDs while enhancing their luminescence efficiency, offering a novel approach for synthesizing water-soluble fluorescent quantum dots. Additionally, the incorporation of an immunomultilayer structure endowed the sensor with high sensitivity, meeting the requirements for trace SCCA detection and providing an effective method for early disease screening.

SCCA and its antibodies were purchased from MedChemExpress Co., Ltd (USA). The following reagents and materials were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai): HAuCl4·3H2O, AgNO3, sodium citrate, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), indium tin oxide (ITO) electrodes, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), tert-butyldimethylchlorosilane, and bovine serum albumin (BSA).

The fluorescence intensity of the sensor was measured using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Electrochemical characterization of the sensor, including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), was performed on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments, Shanghai). The workstation used a three-electrode system as follows: a working electrode (ITO electrode, 2 cm × 0.5 cm), an Ag/AgCl reference (saturated KCl), and a platinum wire auxiliary electrode. The crystal structure of the nanomaterials was analyzed using an Ultima IV X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku Corporation, Japan), and their morphology and structural features were studied with a Zeiss scanning electron microscope (SEM, Carl Zeiss, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI Tecnai G2 F20, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the nanomaterials was conducted with a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

First, 20 g pineapple leaves were dried in an oven at 70 °C for 6 h and then ground into a fine powder using an agate mortar for 1 h. The powder was subjected to pyrolysis in an argon atmosphere at 700 °C in a tube furnace for 3 h, producing a black powder. Next, 3.0 g black powder was added to 10 mL of a mixed acid solution (concentrated sulphuric acid and nitric acid, 3:1, v/v) and heated under reflux in a water bath at 90 °C for 2 h. The mixture was then diluted with 100 mL deionised water and neutralised with 2 mol L−1 sodium hydroxide until the pH reached 7.2. The solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane and dialysed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff: 3500 Da) with 0.05 mol L−1 phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.4) for 4 h, yielding CQDs with reddish fluorescence. The obtained solution was dried to produce a reddish-brown powder. Subsequently, 0.5 g of powder was dispersed in 10 mL deionised water, sonicated for 10 min, and filtered through a 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane to obtain highly stable CQDs.

Next, 10 mL ethylenediamine solution (0.5 mol L−1) was added, and the mixture was transferred to a reaction vessel and heated at 240 °C for 3 h. The product was dialysed (3500 Da) in 0.05 mol L−1 PBS (pH = 7.4) for 5 h. The solution was then evaporated using a vacuum rotary evaporator at 50 °C, and the product was dispersed in 10 mL of deionised water. Then, 10 mg sodium citrate was added, and the solution was heated in a 95 °C water bath for 30 min, followed by the addition of 20 mg AgNO3. The mixture was refluxed at 95 °C for 1.5 h, filtered through a 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane, and dried under vacuum at 70 °C for 5 h, yielding Ag NPs@N/CQDs. Finally, 0.05 g Ag NPs@N/CQDs were ultrasonically dispersed in 5 mL 0.05 mol L−1 PBS (pH = 7.4) for further use.

A solution of 0.15 g HAuCl4·3H2O in 150 mL of deionised water was added to a 250 mL beaker and stirred thoroughly. The solution was heated to boiling, followed by the rapid addition of 3 mL 1.5 % sodium citrate. The mixture was maintained at boiling temperature for 30 min and then cooled to 25 °C. The resulting solution was dialysed (3500 Da) in 0.05 mol L−1 PBS (pH = 7.4) for 3 h. After dialysis, the solution was vacuum-dried at 50 °C to obtain Au NPs. Subsequently, 3 mg Au NPs was dissolved in 10 mL PBS (0.05 mol L−1, pH = 7.4), followed by the sequential addition of 0.5 mL EDC (0.02 mol L−1), 0.5 mL NHS (0.05 mol L−1) and 5 mL SCCA (0.005 g mL−1). The mixture was stirred at 30 °C for 1 h and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min. The product was washed twice with 0.01 mol L−1 tert-butyldimethylsilyl chloride and ultrasonically dispersed in 10 mL PBS (0.05 mol L−1, pH = 7.4), yielding ready-to-use Au NP–labelled SCCA antibody (secondary antibody).

An ITO electrode (2 cm × 0.5 cm) was cleaned by soaking in 10 mL ethanol for 5 min, rinsed with 10 mL deionised water, and air-dried. The electrode was immersed in 10 mL PBS (0.05 mol L−1, pH = 7.4) containing 0.05 g Ag NPs@N/CQDs and 1 mL 0.02 mol L−1 EDC, acting as a crosslinking agent. After stirring for 5 min, the reaction proceeded for 10 min to obtain an Ag NPs@N/CQDs-modified ITO electrode. Next, 2 mL SCCA (0.005 g mL−1, primary antibody), 1 mL EDC (0.02 mol L−1) and 0.5 mL NHS (0.05 mol L−1) were added. The mixture was stirred for 5 min and reacted at 30 °C for 10 min. To block the electrode surface, 2 mL 1 % BSA was added at 4 °C under continuous stirring for 5 min. The ITO electrode was rinsed with PBST (PBS containing 0.05 % Tween-20). After washing, 50 μL PBS (0.05 mol L−1, pH = 7.4) containing different concentrations of SCCA was added to the electrode and incubated for 20 min. Finally, 2 mL Au NPs–labelled secondary antibody was added and incubated at 37 °C for 25 min to form a sandwich immunocomplex. Excess antibodies were removed by washing with PBST, and the ITO electrode was placed in a fluorometer for fluorescence intensity measurement.

The ITO electrode was directly placed in the fluorometer detection cell. The fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength (λex) of 465 nm, with both the excitation and emission slit widths set to 5 nm. For the electrochemical analysis, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed. The modified ITO electrodes were immersed in a solution of 3 × 10−4 mol L−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.5 mol L−1 KCl. CV scans were conducted from − 0.2 to 0.6 V at a scan rate of 0.050 V s−1. EIS measurements were performed at a potential of 0.15 V with a 5 mV amplitude over a frequency range of 100 to 100 kHz.

Four serum samples from different individuals were provided by the authors and their colleagues. Each 10 mL serum sample was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. Then, 50 μL of the supernatant was collected, dried with nitrogen gas, and resolubilized in 50 μL of PBS (0.05 mol L−1, pH = 7.4). Fluorescence intensity measurements were performed according to Sections 2.4 and 2.5. A solution of SCCA in PBS (0.05 mol L−1, pH = 7.4) with a concentration of 250 fg mL−1 was prepared. The samples were spiked with 1 μL, 4 μL, 20 μL, and 40 μL of SCCA solution, respectively. Artificially spiked samples were processed identically to the real samples. The fluorescence intensity of each sample was measured, and the results were substituted into the calibration curve. Each sample was tested five times, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated. To compare with the ELISA method, an additional 500 μL of serum was processed according to the ELISA protocol, as its detection limit was insufficiently low for direct comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Ag NPs@N/CQDs and Au NPs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM was used to determine the morphology of the synthesised Ag NPs@N/CQDs. Core–shell spherical nanoparticles with N/GQDs cores were observed in the TEM image (Fig. 2 A). The particles were uniformly distributed with consistently sized, indicating good dispersion of Ag NPs@N/CQDs. The size distribution of the synthesised Ag NPs@N/CQDs was statistically analysed, as shown in Fig. 2B. The particle size ranges from 20 to 80 nm, with an average particle size of approximately 46 nm. The crystallographic structure of Ag NPs@N/CQDs was analysed by XRD (Fig. 2C). A narrow and low-intensity peak (indicating large particles) appeared at 2θ = 24.6°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of the CQDs. Sharp peaks at 2θ = 37.9°, 44.1°, 64.6°, and 77.1° matched the (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes of Ag NPs, respectively [28]. XPS analysis (Fig. 2D) confirmed the elemental composition: C(1 s) peaks appeared at 284.08 eV (CQDs), while Ag(3d3/2) and Ag(3d5/2) peaks at 368.08 eV and 374.08 eV corresponded to Ag NPs. These results validated the successful synthesis of Ag NPs@N/CQDs. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) revealed the morphology of Au NPs (Fig. 2E), which exhibited uniform spherical shapes. Their size distribution (Fig. 2F) ranged from 2–30 nm with an average diameter of ∼ 15 nm. XRD analysis (Fig. 2G) showed peaks at 2θ = 38.2°, 44.4°, 64.9°, and 77.6°, assigned to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of face-centered cubic Au NPs [29], further confirming their successful synthesis.

The stepwise fabrication of the immunosensor was monitored using CV and EIS in a3 × 10−4 mol L−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] solution. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, the bare ITO electrode (curve a) exhibited a well-defined redox peak, reflecting efficient electron transfer. Upon modification with Ag NPs@N/CQDs (curve b), the peak current decreased slightly, suggesting partial blocking of the electrode surface by the nanocomposite. Subsequent immobilization of the primary SCCA antibody (curve c) caused a further reduction in current, consistent with the insulating nature of proteins hindering electron transfer. This trend intensified after capturing 0.1 pg mL−1 SCCA antigen (curve d) and binding with Au NP-labeled secondary antibodies (curve e), where the progressive current decline confirmed successful layer-by-layer assembly. The EIS results (Fig. 3B) corroborated these observations. The bare ITO electrode (curve a) displayed low charge-transfer resistance, while Ag NPs@N/CQDs modification (curve b) increased charge-transfer resistance. Antibody immobilization (curve c) amplifiedcharge-transfer resistance significantly, as primary antibodies create barriers for K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]diffusion. Subsequent antigen–antibody binding (curves d and e) further elevated impedance. These systematic changes validate the successful fabrication of the sensor.

The optical performance of Ag NPs@N/CQDs were investigated using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S1, Ag NPs@N/CQDs exhibited a strong absorption band at 470 nm. Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra (Fig. S2) revealed a maximum excitation revealed a maximum excitation (λex) at 465 nm (curve a) and a sharp emission (λex) peak at 550 nm (curve b), yielding a large Stokes shift of 85 nm. A larger Stokes displacement means that the emitted light and absorbed light are separated further in the spectrum, thus reducing the problems of spectral overlap and self-absorption and improving the utilization efficiency of photons. The emission profile of Ag NPs@N/CQDs displayed high intensity, symmetry, and a narrow full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM), indicative of uniform particle size and monodispersity. These properties are critical for stable and reproducible fluorescence signals in biosensing applications. The quantum yield (QY) of Ag NPs@N/CQDs was calculated as 0.77 using a reference method [30]. This high QY positions Ag NPs@N/CQDs as an excellent emitter for ultrasensitive detection, where bright and stable fluorescence is essential for quantifying low-abundance biomarkers like SCCA.

The fluorescence quenching of the Ag NPs@N/CQDs on the immunosensor in the presence of SCCA was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, as increasing concentrations of SCCA were adsorbed onto the immunosensor, more secondary antibodies were bound, leading to the attachment of more Au NPs to the sensor. This resulted in a continuous decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the Ag NPs@N/CQDs. This indicates that Au NPs can effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the Ag NPs@N/CQDs on the immunosensor. We propose that the fluorescence quenching arises from energy transfer between Au NPs and Ag NPs@N/CQDs. To investigate this mechanism, we first measured the fluorescence lifetime of Ag NPs@N/CQDs. The results revealed a marked reduction in lifetime from 5.7 ns to 3.5 ns upon introducing Au NPs, consistent with dynamic quenching. Such quenching typically involves two mechanisms [31]: energy transfer (driven by charge interactions between the quencher and excited fluorophore) or electron transfer (requiring two simultaneous conditions: substantial spectral overlap between emission spectrum of donor and absorption spectrum of acceptor, and proximity between donor and acceptor). Further analysis identified a significant overlap between the emission spectrum of Ag NPs@N/CQDs and the absorption spectrum of Au NPs (Fig. 4B). Additionally, zeta potential measurements showed that Ag NPs@N/CQDs possess a positive surface charge (+3.41 mV), while Au NPs exhibit a strongly negative charge (–32.47 mV). The electrostatic attraction between these oppositely charged nanoparticles minimizes their intermolecular distance, enabling direct interaction. Based on this evidence, we conclude that the fluorescence quenching is mediated by energy transfer. Specifically, excited-state Ag NPs@N/CQDs transfer energy to Au NPs, preventing their return to the ground state and thereby suppressing fluorescence emission.

The fluorescence intensity of the immunoassay critically depends on two incubation steps: (1) primary antibody capturing SCCA (0.5 pg mL−1), and (2) SCCA binding with Au NP-labeled secondary antibodies. As shown in Fig. S3, fluorescence intensity progressively declined over 0–30 min (Step 1), plateauing at 20 min as antibody-antigen binding reached saturation. Similarly, Fig. S4 shows continuous fluorescence attenuation during Step 2 (0–40 min), stabilizing at 25 min due to immunocomplex completion. The observed quenching correlates with increased Au NP loading: prolonged incubation enhances SCCA capture and subsequent secondary antibody-Au NP assembly, amplifying energy transfer from Ag NPs@N/CQDs. Optimal incubation times (20 min for Step 1; and 25 min for Step 2) were selected to maximize detection sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of the immunosensor to SCCA
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized conditions, the immunosensor’s fluorescence response to SCCA (0.87–4000 fg mL−1) was quantified. As SCCA concentration increased (Fig. 5 A), fluorescence intensity exhibited concentration-dependent quenching.A good linear relationship is observed between the fluorescence quenching intensity (△F) of the probe and an SCCA concentration (c) of 2 − 4000 fg mL−1. The linear regression equation is F = 0.43c(fg mL−1) + 29.22, r = 0.996 (Fig. 5B), and the detection limit is 0.87 fg mL−1 (D.L. = KSb /a, K = 3). Therefore, this immunosensor offers higher sensitivity for detecting SCCA than existing reported methods (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of immunosensor for SCCA was validated against 12 potential interferents (IgG, α-globulin, β-globulin, γ-globulin, CEA, PSA, serotonin, glucose, fructose, K+, Na+, and Cl−). Fluorescence intensity measurements of 50 fg mL−1 SCCA (F0) and its mixture with 5 pg mL−1 interferents (F1) showed minimal relative deviation (RD% = [(F0 −F 1)/F0 ] × 100 % < 2 %, Fig. S5). This negligible signal variation (<2%) confirms exceptional selectivity for SCCA, even at 100-fold higher interferent concentrations.

The immunosensor exhibited high reproducibility and stability for SCCA detection. Ten independently fabricated sensors tested with 50 fg mL−1 SCCA showed a relative standard deviation (RSD) of ΔF values < 3 % (Fig. S6), demonstrating excellent batch-to-batch consistency. Stability assessments revealed 96.4 % retention of initial fluorescence intensity 30 min post-reaction (Fig. S7), confirming robust operational durability.

Human serum samples were processed and tested according to the method described in Section 2.6, and a spiked recovery experiment was conducted simultaneously. ELISA methods were used for comparison. As shown in Table 1 , the recovery rate of the immunosensor ranged from 94.45 % to 103.43 %, with an RSD of < 3 %.

In this study, a fluorescent composite nanomaterial (Ag NPs@N/CQDs) was synthesized and utilized as thesignal probein an immunosensor. A sandwich immunoassay structure was employed to enable the highly sensitive detection of trace SCCA in human serum. Ag NPs@N/CQDs exhibit stable and intense fluorescence emission spectra along with a high quantum yield. The introduction of the sandwich immunoassay structure provides specific recognition of the target molecule (SCCA) the effectively mitigates interference from non-target species. Consequently, this immunosensor demonstrates high sensitivity and selectivity for trace-level SCCA detection in human serum.

Zhirou Xu: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis. Zhengtong Wu: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xuyang Sun: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the First Clinical Medical School, Hainan Medical University, China, and Department of Ophthalmology, Hainan Provincial Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine, China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114075.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
